content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Search Has Changed Forever
For almost two decades, developers optimized websites for one audience: search engines.
We focused on keywords. We chased backlinks. We improved Core Web Vitals. We optimized metadata.
Everything revolved around Google.
But something fundamental has changed.
Today, millions of users don’t start with a search engine anymore. They ask ChatGPT. They ask Gemini. They ask Perplexity. They ask Claude.
The first answer users see is no longer a list of blue links. It’s an AI-generated response.
That realization completely changed how I think about building websites.
I stopped asking: “How can I rank higher on Google?”
Instead, I started asking: “How can an AI system understand my website with confidence?”
This article explains how I redesigned my portfolio around that idea — and how you can do the same for yours.

SEO Is No Longer Enough
Traditional SEO still matters. Fast loading pages. Good accessibility. Semantic HTML. Internal linking. Structured metadata. None of these disappeared.
But AI systems evaluate websites differently.
They don’t simply rank pages. They summarize them. They connect entities. They identify relationships. They generate answers.
That means your website needs to communicate with both humans and machines.
The old playbook — keyword density, backlink quantity, exact-match anchor text — was built for an era when Google’s crawler was the only machine reading your site. Today, large language models (LLMs) from OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, and others are parsing your content in radically different ways.
They’re not looking for keyword matches. They’re looking for understanding.

My Goal: A Structured Knowledge Source
When ChatGPT, Gemini, or Perplexity encounters my website, I don’t want it to simply crawl another HTML document.
I want it to understand:

• Who I am

• What I build

• Which technologies I use

• Which problems I solve

• Which pages contain authoritative information
In other words: I want my website to become a structured knowledge source.
This isn’t just about being “discoverable.” It’s about being citeable. When an AI generates an answer that includes information from your site, that’s a form of referral that no traditional SEO metric can capture. It’s trust. It’s authority. And it’s earned through clarity, not tricks.

The Architecture of an AI-First Website
Instead of thinking about SEO as a checklist, I started treating the entire website as a knowledge graph.
Every page has a purpose. Every article connects to related topics. Every project reinforces my technical expertise. Everything contributes to one consistent identity.
The result is a website that’s easier for both humans and AI systems to interpret.
Here’s how I built it — layer by layer.
1. Semantic HTML: The Foundation
Instead of relying on generic <div> containers, I use meaningful HTML elements that clearly describe the structure of the page.
AI systems benefit from the same semantic hierarchy that improves accessibility. When a screen reader can navigate your site, so can an AI parser. When your <article> tags properly encapsulate content, an LLM can more easily identify the boundaries of a self-contained piece of information.
What I use:

• <header> for site navigation and branding

• <main> for primary content

• <article> for blog posts and project pages

• <section> for logical content groupings

• <aside> for complementary information

• <footer> for site-wide metadata and links

• <nav> for navigation menus
This isn’t just good practice — it’s essential for AI interpretability.
2. Structured Metadata: Context Before Content
Every page includes descriptive metadata that helps machines understand the context before reading the content.
Titles. Descriptions. Open Graph tags. Canonical URLs. JSON-LD where appropriate.
These signals help AI systems understand what a page is about before they even start parsing the text. Think of it as a table of contents that an AI can read instantly, without having to infer meaning from the content itself.
Key metadata I prioritize:

• Title tags that clearly state the page’s purpose

• Meta descriptions that summarize the content in 150–160 characters

• Open Graph tags for social sharing context

• JSON-LD structured data with appropriate schemas (Person, Article, BlogPosting, FAQPage, etc.)

• Canonical URLs to prevent duplicate content confusion
For my portfolio at gucluyumhe.dev, I implemented these across every page, ensuring that whether an AI reads my homepage, my projects archive, or a specific blog post, it gets immediate context about what it’s looking at.
3. AI-Friendly Content: Answer First, Explain Later
Rather than writing long, meandering introductions, I answer the main question early. Then I progressively add technical depth.
This mirrors how modern AI systems retrieve and summarize information. When an LLM scans your page, it often pays more attention to the opening paragraphs. If you bury your main point in the fifth paragraph, you risk the AI missing it entirely.
My content structure:

• The Answer — Directly address the core question or topic in the first 100–150 words

• The Context — Provide background and why this matters

• The Details — Add technical depth, examples, and evidence

• The Implications — Explain what this means for the reader

• The Call to Action — What should the reader do next?
This inverted pyramid approach isn’t just good for AI — it’s also better for human readers who increasingly scan before committing to a full read.
4. Entity-Based Writing: Beyond Keywords
Instead of stuffing keywords, I focus on entities — the people, technologies, concepts, and organizations that define my expertise.
For example:

• Next.js

• TypeScript

• AI Search / Generative Engine Optimization (GEO)

• Performance

• Software Architecture

• Model Context Protocol (MCP)

• Full-Stack Engineering
These concepts naturally reinforce one another throughout the website. When I write about Next.js architecture, I link to related articles about performance optimization. When I discuss AI agents, I connect to my work on MCP.
This creates a web of interrelated entities that AI systems can traverse. An LLM doesn’t just see isolated keywords — it sees a connected knowledge graph that reinforces your authority across multiple related domains.
5. llms.txt: The Emerging Standard
I also experimented with an llms.txt file. It's an emerging convention designed to provide AI systems with a concise overview of a website and its key resources.
While it’s gaining attention, it’s still experimental, and major platforms haven’t uniformly adopted it. I see it as a low-cost addition rather than a guaranteed ranking signal. Strong content, clear structure, and authoritative pages remain far more important.
That said, the concept is worth watching. As AI systems become more sophisticated, having a machine-readable summary of your entire site could become as standard as robots.txt or sitemap.xml are today.
6. Generative Engine Optimization (GEO)
One of the most important shifts I’ve embraced is the move from traditional SEO to Generative Engine Optimization (GEO).
Traditional SEO was about ranking. GEO is about being cited — ensuring that when an AI generates an answer, your content is the source it references.
Key GEO principles I follow:

• Clarity over cleverness — Write so that an AI can summarize your content without losing meaning

• Structured information — Use lists, tables, and clear hierarchies

• Authoritative sourcing — Cite your own work and connect related ideas

• Consistent terminology — Use the same terms for the same concepts across your site

• Freshness — Keep content updated; AI systems favor current information
I’ve written extensively about these principles on my blog, including articles like “How I Designed and Built My Portfolio: Next.js Architecture for 2026” and “Is the Internet Dying? The Future of Websites in the Age of AI.”

AI Search Is About Trust
One thing became obvious while building this system.
AI doesn’t reward websites simply because they exist. It references websites that are understandable.
Clear architecture. Consistent content. Reliable technical information. Strong topical authority.
Those qualities matter far more than chasing isolated optimization tricks.
Think about it from the AI’s perspective. When a model like ChatGPT or Gemini generates a response, it’s drawing from a vast corpus of training data. But when it needs to answer a specific, current question, it often turns to real-time search results. The sites it chooses to cite are the ones it can parse with confidence.
If your site is a mess of conflicting information, vague statements, and unclear structure, the AI will ignore it. If your site is a clean, authoritative, well-organized knowledge source, the AI will cite it.

Practical Implementation: My Portfolio as a Case Study
Let me walk you through how I applied these principles to my own portfolio at gucluyumhe.dev.
Homepage: Clear Identity, Immediate Context
My homepage immediately establishes who I am and what I do: “20-year-old Full-Stack Engineer focused on building high-performance digital architectures that merge complex logic with human-centric design.”
This isn’t vague marketing copy. It’s a precise statement of identity that an AI can parse and categorize. Within seconds, an LLM knows:

• My name: Ömer Özbay

• My age: 20

• My role: Full-Stack Engineer

• My focus: AI, SaaS, UX

• My philosophy: Human-centric design
Below that, I provide quantifiable credibility markers: “50+ GitHub Repositories” and “2 Yrs Professional Experience.” These aren’t just badges — they’re data points that AI systems can use to assess authority.
Projects Archive: Structured Portfolio
My projects page serves as a structured archive of my work. Each project is presented consistently, with clear titles, descriptions, and technologies used. This consistency is crucial for AI parsing.
When an AI scans my projects, it sees a pattern: project → description → technologies → outcomes. That pattern allows it to summarize my capabilities without having to infer meaning from unstructured text.
Blog: Deep Technical Content
My blog is where the real depth lives. Articles like “Building an AI Agent with Model Context Protocol (MCP): A Practical Guide” and “Securify: Modern Cybersecurity Forensics and Threat Modeling” demonstrate specialized knowledge across multiple domains.
But here’s the key: every blog post follows the same structure I described earlier — answer first, then context, then details, then implications. This consistency means that an AI can parse any post on my site and immediately find the core thesis.

Common Mistakes to Avoid
If you’re building an AI-first website, here are pitfalls I’ve seen — and avoided:
1. Over-Optimizing for Keywords
Keyword stuffing doesn’t work for AI any better than it worked for Google. LLMs are sophisticated enough to recognize when content is artificially padded. Write naturally, focus on entities, and let the keywords emerge organically.
2. Neglecting Mobile Performance
AI systems care about page speed because user experience matters to them too. A slow site is a bad experience, and AI models are trained to prioritize good experiences. Core Web Vitals still matter.
3. Inconsistent Terminology
If you call something “AI Search” on one page and “Generative Engine Optimization” on another, an AI might not connect the two. Pick your terms and stick with them.
4. Ignoring Structured Data
JSON-LD might seem like extra work, but it’s one of the most direct ways to communicate with AI systems. Use it. Every page should have appropriate structured data.
5. Writing for Machines, Not Humans
Remember: AI systems are ultimately serving human users. If your content reads like it was written for a bot, it won’t engage human readers — and AI models will eventually learn to recognize and penalize that. Write for humans first, structure for
